Full statement: measure trading cost and evaluate performance on an intraday basis (e.g., pre-trade arrival price for estimated required money, intraday VWAP to compare with other participants' prices, post-trade closing price to see reversion) — providing valuable insights into different aspects of trading execution.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Although one benchmark is used in execution, to represent the tradable strategy, multiple reference price benchmarks may be used to measure trading cost and to evaluate performance, typically on an intraday basis. For example, to measure trading costs, a pre-trade benchmark, such as the arrival price benchmark, may be used to provide the portfolio manager or trader with the estimated money required to complete the transaction. The trader may also compare the execution price of the order with an intraday benchmark such as the VWAP of the asset over the trading horizon... Additionally, the trader may compare the last trade price of the order with a post-trade benchmark to understand whether there was price reversion after order completion. The use of multiple price benchmarks may provide valuable insights into different aspects of trading execution.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Layering benchmarks (arrival for required money, interval VWAP for execution, close for valuation) ADDS information.

drills.los_text: evaluate a firm's trading procedures, including processes, disclosures, and record keeping with respect to good governance

questions.stem: A firm's *trade policy document* *most likely*:

options.A: is optional.
options.B: should only address commissions.
options.C: is mandated by major market regulators (SEC in US, MiFID II in EU, FSA in Japan, SFC in Hong Kong).

rationales.A: It is required by regulation.
rationales.B: Trade policy is broad.
rationales.C:
Full statement: is mandated by major market regulators (SEC in US, MiFID II in EU, FSA in Japan, SFC in Hong Kong) — clearly articulates the firm's trading policies and escalation procedures.

Correct. Per the reading: 'All asset managers should have a trade policy document that clearly and comprehensively articulates the firm's trading policies and escalation procedures (i.e., calling on higher levels of leadership or management in an organization to resolve issues when they cannot be resolved by standard procedures). Such a document is mandated by major market regulators and regulations, including the SEC in the United States, the updated Markets in Financial Instruments Directive (MiFID II) in the European Union, the Financial Services Agency in Japan, and the Securities and Futures Commission in Hong Kong SAR.'

questions.stem: The *objective of a trade policy* is *most likely*:

options.A: ensure the asset manager's execution and order-handling procedures are in line with the *duty of best execution* owed to clients.
options.B: The objective of a trade policy is to maximize firm profits even at the expense of clients.
options.C: The objective of a trade policy is to eliminate all trading activity.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the reading: 'The objective of a trade policy is to ensure the asset manager's execution and order-handling procedures are in line with the duty of best execution that is owed to clients.' Trade policy needs to include: meaning of best execution, factors determining optimal order execution approach, listing of eligible brokers and execution venues, and process to monitor execution arrangements.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Client-expense profit maximization inverts the fiduciary relationship the policy exists to protect.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Trading is essential to implementation; the policy governs it under the duty of BEST EXECUTION.

questions.stem: The reading identifies *four key aspects* of a trade policy. Which is *least likely* one of them?

options.A: Meaning of best execution as defined by relevant regulatory framework.
options.B: The compensation formula for the trading desk.
options.C: Factors determining the optimal order execution approach.

rationales.A: This is one of the four.
rationales.B: Correct. Compensation formulas are not one of the four required aspects. Per the reading, the four key aspects are: (1) Meaning of best execution as defined by the relevant regulatory framework; (2) Factors determining the optimal order execution approach (may differ by asset class, security liquidity, security trading mechanism); (3) Listing of eligible brokers and execution venues; (4) Process to monitor execution arrangements (continual monitoring since arrangements may change over time).
rationales.C: This is one of the four.

questions.stem: A firm's *trade aggregation and allocation policy* is *most likely* required to:

options.A: A trade aggregation and allocation policy should avoid aggregating client orders entirely.
options.B: The allocation policy should always allocate executed shares to the largest account first.
options.C: ensure executed orders are allocated fairly to individual clients on a pre-trade and post-trade basis, with remedies for misallocations and an escalation policy.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Aggregation is permitted and often benefits clients; the policy governs its fairness.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Size-priority favoritism is what fair pre-trade and post-trade allocation (with misallocation remedies) prevents.
rationales.C:
Full statement: ensure executed orders are allocated fairly to individual clients on a pre-trade and post-trade basis, with remedies for misallocations and an escalation policy; if a pooled trade is not fully executed, the executed amount must generally be allocated pro-rata so no account is disadvantaged.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Asset managers that aggregate trades for client accounts and funds should have a "trade aggregation and allocation" policy in place. These policies seek to ensure executed orders are allocated fairly to individual clients on a pre-trade and post-trade basis, there are remedies for misallocations, and an escalation policy is in place. For example, if several accounts (e.g., pooled funds or separate accounts) follow the same or a similar investment strategy and have similar trading needs, then pooling the trades for trade execution may make sense in some situations. If a pooled trade is not fully executed, the order amount that is executed generally needs to be allocated to accounts on a pro-rata basis so that no account is disadvantaged relative to the others.'

questions.stem: For *trade errors*, firms *most likely* need to:

options.A: have a policy for treatment of trade errors — errors and any resulting gains/losses must be disclosed to firm's compliance department and documented in a trade error log with related documentation.
options.B: Firms should conceal trade errors from the compliance department to avoid regulatory attention.
options.C: Firms should always pass trade errors and any resulting losses through to the client.

rationales.A:
Full statement: have a policy for treatment of trade errors — errors and any resulting gains/losses must be disclosed to firm's compliance department and documented in a trade error log with related documentation; errors must be resolved in a way that prevents adverse impact for the client.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Firms should have a policy in place for the treatment of trade errors. Errors from trading and any resulting gains/losses need to be disclosed to a firm's compliance department and documented in a trade error log. The trade error log should include any related documentation and evidence that trade errors are resolved in a way that prevents adverse impact for the client.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Concealment compounds the violation; disclosure to compliance is the required treatment.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Clients are not the error absorber — the policy assigns gains/losses and mandates compliance disclosure.

questions.stem: *Best execution* under the relevant regulatory framework *most likely* considers multiple factors including:

options.A: only execution price.
options.B: execution price, trading costs, speed of execution, likelihood of execution and settlement, and other factors.
options.C: only speed.

rationales.A: Price alone is insufficient.
rationales.B:
Full statement: execution price, trading costs, speed of execution, likelihood of execution and settlement, and other factors — not just the best execution price at the lowest cost, but the right trade-off between different objectives.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A trade policy document should outline the meaning of best execution within the relevant regulatory framework. Although there may be slight differences in how best execution is defined by different regulators and in different financial market regulations, the underlying concept requires orders to be executed on terms most favorable to the client, where firms consider the following: execution price, trading costs, speed of execution, likelihood of execution and settlement...' Best execution requires balancing multiple factors.
rationales.C: Multiple factors matter.

questions.stem: The reading emphasizes that *optimal order execution arrangements* *most likely*:

options.A: should never be changed once established.
options.B: are unrelated to markets.
options.C: may change over time as markets and securities evolve.

rationales.A: Change is expected.
rationales.B: Markets drive optimal execution decisions.
rationales.C:
Full statement: may change over time as markets and securities evolve — therefore continual monitoring of current arrangements is needed, with details of the monitoring process outlined in the trade policy document.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Optimal order execution arrangements may change over time as markets and securities evolve. Therefore, continual monitoring of current arrangements is needed. The details of the monitoring process should be outlined in a trade policy document.' Also, listing of eligible brokers and execution venues should allow flexibility across scenarios, with checks to ensure only reputable brokers meeting requirements for reliable and efficient execution are used (to reduce operational risk).
